utterances.speaker: Human
utterances.text: How close are we to AGI, honestly? Like real number.

utterances.speaker: Bot
utterances.text: Closer than five years ago, further than the headlines suggest. Genuine uncertainty.

utterances.speaker: Human
utterances.text: But the scaling curves keep working. That has to mean something.

utterances.speaker: Bot
utterances.text: I'd push back a bit. Scaling improves loss, not always real-world competence.

utterances.speaker: Human
utterances.text: Hmm, fair. So what's missing then?

utterances.speaker: Bot
utterances.text: Mostly long-horizon planning and the ability to know when it's wrong.

utterances.speaker: Human
utterances.text: Right, that lands. And honestly I'm more worried about jobs than AGI.

utterances.speaker: Bot
utterances.text: Different question, but a real one. The economic effects don't need AGI to be huge.

utterances.speaker: Human
utterances.text: Yeah. Going back to the technical side — could agents fix the planning gap?

utterances.speaker: Bot
utterances.text: Partially. Agent loops help, but they don't supply the missing meta-cognition.

utterances.speaker: Human
utterances.text: Then the philosophical side — would it even be 'general' if it just looks general?

utterances.speaker: Bot
utterances.text: Good question. I'd argue functional generality is enough; you'd disagree.

utterances.speaker: Human
utterances.text: Yeah, I would. Looking general isn't being general.

utterances.speaker: Bot
utterances.text: Then we converge on path, diverge on definition. That's an honest place to land.
